notes: Deep dive on Texas data center opposition. TX has 39 entries but is the largest US datacenter market. Cross-referenced all 39 existing TX entries against comprehensive web research. Found 5 genuinely new entries and 10 status/enrichment updates for existing entries.

new_entries.summary: Residents of the tiny community of Blue in Lee County, ~50 miles east of Austin, organized 'Move the Gas Plant' to fight a 1,200 MW Sandow Lakes Energy natural gas power plant that would serve nearby data centers including North America's largest Bitcoin mining facility. The group formally requested a TCEQ hearing on the air pollution permit (authorizing 460 tons/year ammonia, 153 tons soot, 76 tons sulfuric acid, 18 tons hazardous air pollutants), but TCEQ denied the hearing request in 45 seconds and issued the permit in October 2025. The group advocates relocating the plant to the former Alcoa industrial site 15 miles away. The Texas Energy Fund also denied Sandow Lakes' $295M loan application.

Hays County Judge Ruben Becerra proposed a 30-day moratorium on permits for developments using more than 20,000 gallons of water per day, directly targeting data centers amid historic aquifer lows. At a Feb. 24 commissioners meeting, ~48 speakers supported the moratorium, but commissioners tabled it due to legal concerns after a letter from state Sen. Paul Bettencourt warned counties lack constitutional authority to impose development moratoriums. Becerra vowed to continue the fight. This is separate from the earlier Hays County vote on a broader data center development moratorium that also failed. Water advocates have identified 5 potential data centers in the region they plan to protest.

new_entries.jurisdiction: Texas Hill Country / Permian Basin (transmission line)
new_entries.project_name: Permian Basin Reliability Plan / Howard-Solstice 765kV Transmission Line
new_entries.summary: A $13 billion Permian Basin Reliability Plan to build Texas' first 765-kilovolt transmission line through the Hill Country is facing organized opposition from a coalition of landowners, conservation advocates, and lawmakers. The line would carry power to serve expanding data center demand in West Texas. Rural landowners across Central and West Texas are mobilizing over concerns about environmental harm to beloved landscapes and rivers, property rights, public health, and higher costs for ratepayers. Burnet and Llano counties are in the impact zone. The plan is at final stages of administrative review with construction potentially starting in 2027.

opposition_groups: State Republican Executive Committee of Texas
new_entries.summary: The Texas State Republican Executive Committee passed a near-unanimous resolution calling for 'rigorous independent assessments' of proposed data center projects and their impact on grid reliability and water shortages before final approval. The resolution also called for a pause on 'open loop' data centers (which use the most water for cooling), as well as oversight and local control measures. This reflects growing bipartisan concern about data centers in Texas, where the state is estimating $1 billion in annual losses from data center tax breaks.

Hood County commissioners voted 3-2 to reject a data center moratorium for the second time in February 2026, sparking resident walkouts and calls for commissioner resignations. Commissioners cited lack of legal authority after a letter from state Sen. Paul Bettencourt warned that counties cannot impose development moratoriums. The county requested a formal opinion from AG Ken Paxton on whether they have authority to enact such a moratorium. Commissioners also committed to exploring water protection codes at their March 10 meeting, citing a section of Texas government code giving them authority to protect Lake Granbury as a recreational area. At least 6 data center projects are proposed in the county.

updates.summary: Hood County commissioners voted 3-2 on Feb. 10 to reject a moratorium on data center development after receiving a letter from state Sen. Paul Bettencourt (R-Houston) warning that counties lack constitutional or statutory authority to impose development moratoriums. The county attorney also cited the letter to AG Paxton. A second moratorium vote on Feb. 24 also failed 3-2, sparking resident walkouts and calls for commissioner resignations. The county has requested a formal AG opinion and committed to exploring water protection codes under existing authority to protect Lake Granbury. At least 6 data center projects are proposed in the county.

InfraKey Capital's proposed $10 billion, 520-acre data center campus (L2D2 - Lacy Lakeview Data District) near Elm Mott in McLennan County continues to face strong organized opposition. Lacy Lakeview City Council voted 6-1 to approve a non-binding MOU with InfraKey and separately approved a resolution to consider future annexation requests for the site. Opposition has circulated petitions with ~3,000 signatures, organized through the 'Stop the data center on Taylor Lane' Facebook group (~2,500 members), and held regular strategy meetings attended by state Rep. Pat Curry. Plans call for up to 925 MW (expandable to 1.2 GW). Annexation efforts continue despite opposition from neighboring communities of Ross and Gholson.
opposition_groups: Stop the data center on Taylor Lane

updates.summary: CyrusOne's 400 MW data center under construction just south of Lake Whitney dam in Bosque County continues to draw opposition. Nearly 6,000 people have signed a 'Save Lake Whitney' petition over concerns that the data center and a planned water reallocation (Tentatively Selected Plan #6 to lower the conservation pool to 512 feet) could drop lake levels. At a January town hall, residents questioned developers about water use, noise, tax revenue, and long-term impact. CyrusOne is seeking well water permits from the Middle Trinity Groundwater Conservation District and has filed for two additional facilities (DFW17B, DFW17C). The project is projected to increase Bosque County tax revenue by 120% ($70M over 30 years).

Misae Investments' proposed 2,742-acre Mojo Energy Hub near Stockdale in Wilson County faces escalating opposition. In less than 5 days, 1,385 verified Wilson County residents signed a petition through the newly formed 'Protect Wilson County Water' organization. Residents packed the Wilson County Courthouse on Feb. 23. The project originally referenced 2 million+ gallons/day of water access but the company's website has since been revised to claim 15,000 gallons/day. Project includes three components: a data center, on-site solar energy, and a gas plant. All three are anticipated to reach commercial operation by June 2028.

The legal battle over MSB Global's massive AI data center in Sulphur Springs intensified. On Feb. 10, Hopkins County District Court Judge Moore issued a temporary injunction blocking power system construction at the site, preserving the 5,000-acre property while the lawsuit between the City of Sulphur Springs and Vistra Corp./Luminant Mining proceeds. Vistra claims a deed restriction prevents commercial energy production or storage on the land. The judge dismissed portions of the city's claims based on fraudulent concealment but denied the city's motion to dismiss Vistra's counterclaims. The Texas AG's office has sided with the city, arguing Luminant has placed restrictive deeds across the state. The city manager has been subpoenaed to testify. Trial date has not been confirmed in available reporting.

Pacifico Energy's GW Ranch project in Pecos County received the largest air pollution permit in the country from TCEQ, authorizing 7.65 GW of gas-fired power generation for the off-grid data center complex. The permit allows release of 12,000+ tons/year of regulated air pollutants (soot, ammonia, carbon monoxide, VOCs) and up to 33 million tons/year of greenhouse gases (~5% of Canada's total annual emissions). Neighbors organized as 'Move the Gas Plant' (this appears to be the Pecos County group, distinct from the Lee County group of the same name) and formally requested a TCEQ hearing, which was denied. On March 13, 2026, the US Senate Environment and Public Works Committee sent a letter to Pacifico's CEO questioning the project's environmental impact. Construction expected to begin Q1 2026.

Hood County commissioners gave conditional 4-1 approval to Sailfish Development's Comanche Circle concept plan for a ~2,600-acre data center near Tolar (pop. 1,300) on Jan. 13, but required a revised plan addressing drainage, wastewater management, landscape impact, and power generation within 45 days (due Feb. 27). As of early March 2026, Texas Scorecard reported that Hood County rejected the revised concept plan. Commissioners plan to revisit data center developments at the March 24 meeting.

Fort Worth City Council approved initial rezoning of ~160 acres for Black Mountain Power's $10 billion data center campus in southeast Fort Worth (between Forest Hill and Everman), but has twice delayed voting on an additional 80-acre rezoning expansion. At the Feb. 10 meeting, Council member Chris Nettles delayed the vote pending an Oncor infrastructure impact report. On March 11, Black Mountain itself requested a further delay to complete its own infrastructure impact report. Residents frustrated with water usage, pollution, and lack of communication. Black Mountain held a private neighborhood meeting on March 12, with residents demanding carbon footprint details. Next votes: zoning commission April 8, council May 12. Nearby cities of Forest Hill and Everman have raised concerns and requested more communication.

San Antonio City Council is moving to update its Unified Development Code to specifically regulate data centers for the first time. Council member Ric Galvan proposed categorizing data centers as 'general industrial' use requiring specific zoning. Mayor Gina Ortiz Jones pushed for expedited amendments rather than waiting for the regular 2027 UDC update cycle, saying 'These companies are not waiting on our timelines.' CPS Energy currently powers 21 data centers with 59 more in planning stages; 11 projects totaling 1,746 MW expected online in 5-7 years. Data centers currently account for 0.1% of drinkable water (could grow to 0.3%) and 1.25% of recycled water (could grow to 12%). Internal UDC review to begin mid-2026. Microsoft alone has $1.4B+ in data center projects in the SA area.

The PUCT is advancing multiple SB 6 implementation rulemakings. On March 12, 2026, PUCT voted to publish draft rule 16 TAC Section 25.194 establishing interconnection standards for new loads of 75 MW or greater (Project 58481); comments due April 17. Parallel rulemakings include: net-metering for co-located generation (Project 58479, at proposal-for-adoption stage), voluntary demand reduction program (Project 58482, scoping started Jan 2026), and transmission cost allocation study (Project 58484, examining whether cost-sharing should change given large load influx). ERCOT's large-load interconnection queue has nearly quadrupled to 233 GW, with 70%+ from data centers. ERCOT has contracted McKinsey to assist with queue improvements.
